text: Transition metal contamination that arises during conventional atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) has long been a significant problem in contemporary polymer science. Here we present a pioneering example of a simple, metal-free ATRP process for the preparation of well-defined copolymers that is enabled by light activation and an organic small-molecule photoredox catalyst. Employing a highly reducing phenothiazine-derived catalyst under light activation affords quantitative vinyl monomer activation and deactivation that is associated with precise control over polymer molecular weight, polydispersity, and end group functionality. The resulting polymers have a predicted M N (based on [M 0 /[I 0 ])) and are free of residual contaminants that need to be removed for example in the case of heterogeneous transition metal catalysts. Furthermore, they can be used to produce a variety of block copolymers in a simple manner, while opening up numerous new possibilities for applying controlled radical polymerization (CRP) to a wide range of synthetic scenarios. It is also envisioned that the organic photoredox catalysts that are utilized here as well as elsewhere in small molecule and polymer chemistry will open a wide range of new opportunities, particularly for applications where transition metal contaminants cannot be tolerated such as in bio-materials and microelectronics applications.
